candidates.description: Thermal energy of the bulk electron population as a function of normalized toroidal flux coordinate in the core plasma
candidates.reason: qualifier=electron, base=temperature; fundamental electron thermal quantity
candidates.docs_description: Mean thermal energy of the bulk electron population per degree of freedom, defined through the second moment of the electron velocity distribution and equal to two-thirds the average electron kinetic energy.
candidates.documentation:
The electron temperature $T_e$ characterizes the mean thermal energy of the electron population under the assumption of a near-Maxwellian velocity distribution. It is the fundamental scalar measure of electron thermal content and controls collisional coupling, radiation losses, and energy transport throughout the plasma.

The defining relation expresses $T_e$ through the second velocity moment of the electron distribution function $f_e(\mathbf{v})$:

$$
T_e = \frac{m_e}{3 n_e} \int |\mathbf{v} - \mathbf{u}_e|^2 f_e(\mathbf{v}) \, d^3v
$$

where $m_e$ is the electron mass, $n_e$ is the electron number density (see [electron_density](name:electron_density)), and $\mathbf{u}_e$ is the electron fluid velocity; with $T_e$ in eV, Boltzmann's constant is absorbed into the unit conversion $1\;\text{eV} = 11605\;\text{K}$.

Thomson scattering and electron cyclotron emission provide the primary profile measurements, while soft X-ray spectroscopy offers complementary line-integrated estimates. The [electron_pressure](name:electron_pressure) follows directly from $T_e$ via $p_e = n_e T_e$, where the pressure gradient drives diamagnetic and neoclassical transport.

Typical core values span 1–30 keV in tokamak H-mode discharges, falling to 10–500 eV near the separatrix and scrape-off layer. The ratio $T_e / T_i$, where $T_i$ is the [ion_temperature](name:ion_temperature), governs the degree of interspecies thermal equilibration and is a key indicator of auxiliary heating dominance.

candidates.description: Ion temperature for a given species in the core plasma, averaged over charge states when multiple charge states are present.
candidates.reason: qualifier=ion, base=temperature; species-level thermal energy averaged over charge states
candidates.docs_description: Thermal energy per degree of freedom of the ion population, expressed as a temperature via the Boltzmann relation, averaged over charge states when multiple charge states are present.
candidates.documentation:
The ion temperature $T_i$ quantifies the mean thermal kinetic energy of the ion population, characterizing the second moment of the ion velocity distribution under conditions close to a local Maxwellian. For plasmas containing multiple ion species or charge states, $T_i$ represents a charge-state-averaged value for the relevant species population.

The defining relation connects ion temperature to ion thermal pressure $p_i$ and number density $n_i$:

$$
T_i = \frac{p_i}{n_i k_B}
$$

where $n_i$ is the ion number density, $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant, and the mean thermal kinetic energy per ion follows as $\langle E_i \rangle = \frac{3}{2} k_B T_i$.

Ion temperature is measured primarily by charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy (CXRS), which infers $T_i$ from the Doppler broadening of impurity emission lines resulting from charge exchange with injected neutral beams. Neutron emission spectroscopy provides an independent estimate in fusion-relevant plasmas by analyzing the energy spectrum of emitted neutrons.

Core $T_i$ values in tokamak H-mode plasmas range from 5-20 keV, pedestal values from 1-4 keV, and scrape-off layer values fall below 100 eV. Under strong neutral beam heating, $T_i$ can substantially exceed the [electron temperature](name:electron_temperature) $T_e$; the two populations equilibrate on the ion-electron Coulomb collision timescale. Ion thermal pressure is recovered directly as $p_i = n_i k_B T_i$, linking this quantity to the [ion pressure](name:ion_pressure) and to pressure-gradient-driven transport.

candidates.description: Upper triangularity (δ_upper) of each flux surface, characterizing the asymmetric D-shaped deformation of the upper plasma cross-section.
candidates.reason: qualifier=upper, base=triangularity, locus=of flux_surface (intrinsic shape property of the surface contour)
candidates.docs_description: Upper triangularity of a flux surface measures the inward radial displacement of the uppermost point of the poloidal cross-section relative to the flux surface geometric center, normalized by the minor radius.
candidates.documentation:
Upper triangularity $\delta_u$ is a dimensionless shape parameter that captures the D-shaped deformation of the upper half of a flux surface's poloidal cross-section. Unlike the average triangularity, the upper and lower values can differ substantially in diverted configurations where the plasma shape lacks up-down symmetry, particularly near the separatrix.

The upper triangularity is defined as:

$$
\delta_u = \frac{R_{\rm geo} - R_{\rm top}}{a}
$$

where $R_{\rm geo} = (R_{\rm max} + R_{\rm min})/2$ is the major radius of the flux surface geometric center, $R_{\rm top}$ is the major radius at the point of maximum vertical coordinate $Z$ on the surface, and $a = (R_{\rm max} - R_{\rm min})/2$ is the flux surface minor radius.

Equilibrium reconstruction determines $\delta_u$ by locating the upper extremal point on each reconstructed flux contour and computing its radial offset from the geometric center, normalized by the minor radius.

Values range from near zero at the magnetic axis to $0.3$–$0.6$ at the separatrix for conventional D-shaped H-mode plasmas; reverse-triangularity configurations yield negative values. For a single-null lower-diverted configuration, $\delta_u$ at the separatrix is typically smaller than [lower_triangularity_of_flux_surface](name:lower_triangularity_of_flux_surface). Together with [elongation_of_flux_surface](name:elongation_of_flux_surface) and [squareness_of_flux_surface](name:squareness_of_flux_surface), this parameter forms part of the standard basis for characterizing flux surface geometry across the plasma cross-section.

candidates.description: Total (thermal plus non-thermal) pressure of an ion species, summed over charge states.
candidates.reason: qualifier=ion, base=pressure; total pressure including thermal and fast contributions
candidates.docs_description: Total pressure of the ion population, encompassing both thermal and non-thermal (fast-ion) contributions summed across all ionic charge states.
candidates.documentation:
Ion pressure is the total mechanical pressure exerted by the ion population, combining contributions from ions in Maxwellian thermal equilibrium and non-thermal populations — such as fast ions generated by auxiliary heating or fusion reactions — summed over all charge states.

The total ion pressure combines thermal and fast-ion components:

$$
p_i = n_i k_B T_i + p_{i,\mathrm{fast}}
$$

where $n_i$ is the ion number density, $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant, $T_i$ is the [ion temperature](name:ion_temperature), and $p_{i,\mathrm{fast}}$ captures contributions from non-thermal ion populations.

The thermal component is inferred from charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy, which yields $T_i$ and ion density profiles, while fast-ion pressure contributions are estimated from neutral particle analyzers, neutron rate measurements, or transport modeling constrained by MHD equilibrium reconstruction.

Typical core values in H-mode tokamak plasmas range from 10–200 kPa, with the ion pressure often comparable to the [electron_pressure](name:electron_pressure) at high auxiliary heating power. The sum $p_e + p_i$ gives the total plasma pressure that governs MHD stability through the normalized plasma beta, and its radial gradient drives the ion heat flux across flux surfaces.

candidates.description: Poloidal beta: volume-averaged plasma pressure normalized to poloidal magnetic pressure, βp = 4∫p dV / (R₀ μ₀ Ip²)
candidates.reason: projection=poloidal component, base=beta; βp is the canonical poloidal variant referencing poloidal field pressure
candidates.docs_description: Dimensionless ratio of volume-averaged plasma pressure to poloidal magnetic field pressure, normalized by the poloidal field generated by the total plasma current.
candidates.documentation:
Poloidal beta $\beta_p$ quantifies plasma confinement efficiency relative to the poloidal magnetic field generated by the plasma current $I_p$ itself, rather than by the externally applied toroidal field. It directly characterizes the self-organization of the plasma equilibrium and couples pressure balance to the current-driven poloidal field.

For a circular cross-section the defining relation is:

$$
\beta_p = \frac{4 \int p \, dV}{\mu_0 I_p^2 R_0}
$$

where $p$ is the total plasma pressure summed over all species, $\mu_0$ is the permeability of free space, $I_p$ is the total [plasma current](name:plasma_current), and $R_0$ is the geometric major radius. An equivalent form is $\beta_p = 2\mu_0 \langle p \rangle / B_\theta^2(a)$, where $\langle p \rangle$ is the volume-averaged pressure and $B_\theta(a)$ is the poloidal magnetic field evaluated at the plasma boundary.

Poloidal beta enters several central equilibrium and transport relations: the Shafranov shift scales as $\Delta/a \propto (\beta_p + l_i/2)\epsilon$, linking $\beta_p$ to the [internal inductance](name:li) $l_i$ and inverse aspect ratio $\epsilon = a/R_0$; the neoclassical bootstrap current fraction scales as $f_{\rm bs} \propto \sqrt{\epsilon}\,\beta_p$, making $\beta_p$ a central figure of merit for advanced steady-state scenarios.

Inferred from equilibrium reconstruction by combining magnetic diagnostic data (flux loops, Rogowski coils) with kinetic pressure profiles obtained from Thomson scattering and charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy. In H-mode discharges $\beta_p$ typically ranges from 0.5 to 2.0; advanced steady-state scenarios targeting high bootstrap fraction aim for $\beta_p > 1.5$.

candidates.description: Magnetic shear s = (ρ_tor/q)·(dq/dρ_tor), quantifying the local radial variation of the safety factor q.
candidates.reason: base=magnetic_shear (registered physical_base); dimensionless radial derivative of q normalized by ρ_tor/q
candidates.docs_description: Dimensionless normalized radial gradient of the safety factor q, measuring how rapidly field-line pitch angles vary across radially adjacent flux surfaces.
candidates.documentation:
Magnetic shear measures the normalized radial gradient of the [safety factor](name:safety_factor) $q$ and is a central parameter in MHD stability theory and turbulent-transport modeling. Positive shear characterizes conventional configurations where $q$ increases outward; negative shear arises when $q$ decreases outward, a regime exploited to form internal transport barriers.

The defining expression is:

$$
s = \frac{\rho_{\rm tor}}{q} \frac{dq}{d\rho_{\rm tor}}
$$

where $\rho_{\rm tor}$ is the toroidal flux radius (the square root of the normalized toroidal magnetic flux) and $q(\rho_{\rm tor})$ is the local [safety factor](name:safety_factor). This normalization ensures $s$ is dimensionless; $s = 0$ on the magnetic axis because $dq/d\rho_{\rm tor}$ vanishes faster than $1/\rho_{\rm tor}$ as $\rho_{\rm tor} \to 0$.

The shear profile is derived by numerically differentiating $q(\rho_{\rm tor})$ from equilibrium reconstruction; motional-Stark-effect polarimetry and Faraday-rotation measurements provide internal constraints on $q$ that propagate directly into $s$.

In H-mode plasmas $s$ is close to zero on axis, grows to 0.5–1.5 at mid-radius, and reaches 2–4 near the plasma edge. Reversed-shear configurations achieve a negative minimum in the range $-1$ to $-3$; the $s = 0$ surface marks the location of the minimum-$q$ flux surface and is associated with reduced turbulent transport and improved energy confinement.

candidates.description: Ratio of toroidal to poloidal field-line turns per flux surface; fundamental MHD stability and equilibrium parameter
candidates.reason: base=safety_factor (registered physical_base); dimensionless; positive when B_phi and I_p share the same toroidal direction, yielding positive winding number (COCOS q_like)
candidates.docs_description: Number of toroidal transits completed by a magnetic field line for each complete poloidal circuit on a given flux surface, governing the helical pitch of field lines and MHD stability thresholds.
candidates.documentation:
The safety factor $q$ measures the pitch of magnetic field lines winding around a toroidal flux surface: it equals the number of times a field line traverses the torus in the toroidal direction per complete poloidal circuit. This ratio determines whether low-order rational resonances ($q = m/n$, with integers $m$ and $n$) arise on a given flux surface, which is the prerequisite for driving MHD instabilities such as tearing modes and kink modes.

The canonical definition connects $q$ to the toroidal and poloidal magnetic fluxes:

$$
q(\psi) = \frac{d\Phi_\mathrm{tor}}{d\Phi_\mathrm{pol}}
$$

where $\Phi_\mathrm{tor}$ is the toroidal magnetic flux enclosed by the flux surface labeled by poloidal flux $\psi$, and $\Phi_\mathrm{pol}$ is the corresponding poloidal flux. In the large-aspect-ratio limit this reduces to $q \approx r B_\phi / (R B_\theta)$, where $r$ is the local minor radius, $R$ the major radius, $B_\phi$ the toroidal field, and $B_\theta$ the poloidal field magnitude on the flux surface.

The $q$ profile is inferred from magnetohydrodynamic equilibrium reconstruction combining external magnetic measurements with internal pitch-angle constraints such as motional Stark effect polarimetry or Faraday rotation. The [magnetic shear](name:magnetic_shear) $s = (r/q)(dq/dr)$ is the logarithmic radial derivative of $q$ and characterizes how rapidly field-line pitch varies across flux surfaces, entering both ideal MHD stability criteria and micro-instability drive.

In conventional H-mode tokamak discharges, $q_0 \approx 1$ on axis and $q_{95} = 3$–5 at the 95% poloidal flux surface. Regions with $q < 1$ are prone to sawtooth oscillations, while neoclassical tearing modes nucleate preferentially at rational surfaces $q = 3/2$ and $q = 2$. The [poloidal_magnetic_flux](name:poloidal_magnetic_flux) profile fully determines $q$ in axisymmetric equilibria.

candidates.description: Total electron number density including thermal and non-thermal contributions, as a function of the radial coordinate.
candidates.reason: qualifier=electron, base=density; covers all cross-IDS siblings measuring electron number density
candidates.docs_description: Number of free electrons per unit volume in the plasma, encompassing thermal and non-thermal (fast) electron populations.
candidates.documentation:
The electron number density $n_e$ is the number of free electrons per unit volume in the plasma, summing over all electron populations including thermal and non-thermal contributions.

Under the quasineutrality approximation, which holds throughout the bulk plasma on length scales much larger than the Debye length $\lambda_D$, $n_e$ is related to the ion charge populations by:

$$
n_e = \sum_i Z_i n_i
$$

where $Z_i$ is the charge number and $n_i$ is the number density of ion species $i$. Deviations from strict quasineutrality arise only on scales comparable to $\lambda_D$, which are negligible for bulk transport purposes.

Electron density is routinely inferred from Thomson scattering, microwave interferometry, and reflectometry across the radial profile; Langmuir probes access edge and scrape-off-layer values. Together with [electron_temperature](name:electron_temperature), it determines [electron_pressure](name:electron_pressure) via $p_e = n_e k_B T_e$, where $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant.

Core values span $1 \times 10^{19}$–$5 \times 10^{20}$ m$^{-3}$ in present-day tokamaks; edge and scrape-off-layer values are 10–100 times lower.

candidates.description: Total electron pressure combining thermal and non-thermal (fast) electron contributions as a function of normalized toroidal flux coordinate.
candidates.reason: qualifier=electron, base=pressure; total (thermal+fast) electron partial pressure on radial grid
candidates.docs_description: Isotropic pressure of the electron population in the plasma, equal to the product of electron number density and electron temperature for a Maxwellian velocity distribution.
candidates.documentation:
The electron pressure $p_e$ quantifies the isotropic stress contribution of the electron species to the total plasma pressure, appearing as a gradient-driven force in the electron momentum balance and as a compressive work term in the electron energy equation. When a non-thermal (suprathermal or fast) electron population is present, the total electron pressure receives additional contributions from the kinetic second-moment integral over the non-Maxwellian tails of the distribution function.

For a Maxwellian electron distribution, the governing relation is:

$$
p_e = n_e k_B T_e
$$

where $n_e$ is the electron number density, $T_e$ is the [electron temperature](name:electron_temperature) expressed in energy units, and $k_B$ is Boltzmann's constant.

In practice, $p_e$ is computed from independent profiles of electron density and [electron temperature](name:electron_temperature), with Thomson scattering providing both quantities simultaneously in a single diagnostic pass; electron cyclotron emission combined with interferometry offers a complementary route for time-resolved profile reconstruction. Equilibrium reconstruction cross-checks the result by integrating the total pressure gradient in the magnetohydrodynamic force balance.

Typical core values in H-mode tokamak discharges range from 10-100 kPa, peaking on axis and decreasing to near zero at the plasma boundary. The electron contribution equals or exceeds the [ion_pressure](name:ion_pressure) in regimes with dominant auxiliary electron heating, making $p_e$ a primary driver of the bootstrap current and of electron-channel energy transport.

candidates.description: Total toroidal magnetic field evaluated at the magnetic axis position
candidates.reason: projection=toroidal component, base=magnetic_field, locus=at magnetic_axis (field value sampled at a point, Rule 18 _at_)
candidates.docs_description: Toroidal component of the magnetic field evaluated at the magnetic axis, the innermost point of the nested flux surfaces where the gradient of poloidal magnetic flux vanishes.
candidates.documentation:
The toroidal magnetic field at the magnetic axis, $B_{\phi,0}$, is the value of $B_\phi = \mathbf{B} \cdot \hat{\phi}$ at the unique point inside the plasma where $\nabla\psi = 0$. It sets the fundamental field-strength reference scale for ion gyrofrequency normalization, Alfvén velocity, and plasma beta calculations in tokamak equilibria.

In an axisymmetric equilibrium, the poloidal current function $F(\psi) = R B_\phi$ is constant on each flux surface. Evaluating at the magnetic axis flux value $\psi_0$ gives:

$$
B_{\phi,0} = \frac{F(\psi_0)}{R_\mathrm{axis}}
$$

where $R_\mathrm{axis}$ is the major radius of the [magnetic axis](name:major_radius_of_magnetic_axis) and $F(\psi_0)$ is the poloidal current function on the innermost flux surface. Because the magnetic axis is displaced inboard of the geometric center by the Shafranov shift, $B_{\phi,0}$ exceeds the vacuum toroidal field at the geometric axis; the vacuum approximation gives $B_{\phi,0} \approx B_\mathrm{vac}\, R_\mathrm{geo} / R_\mathrm{axis}$, where $R_\mathrm{geo}$ is the geometric axis major radius.

This quantity is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction using external magnetic measurements, optionally constrained by internal measurements such as motional Stark effect polarimetry or neutron emissivity profiles. Typical on-axis values range from 1-4 T in medium-sized tokamaks and 4-6 T in high-field devices.

Sign convention: Positive when $B_\phi$ points in the direction of increasing toroidal angle $\phi$.

candidates.description: Total ion number density (thermal plus non-thermal) for a given species, summed over charge states
candidates.reason: subject=ion, base=density; total (thermal+non-thermal) ion number density summed over charge states
candidates.docs_description: Total number of ions per unit volume, summed across all ion species and charge states present in the plasma.
candidates.documentation:
Ion density is the number of ions per unit volume, quantifying the total ionic particle content at a given spatial location. It is a fundamental state variable entering the particle balance, MHD equilibrium, and transport equations governing the ion population.

In a quasi-neutral plasma the ion density is related to the [electron density](name:electron_density) through the charge-weighted sum over all ion species $s$:

$$
n_e \approx \sum_s Z_s \, n_s
$$

where $Z_s$ is the charge number and $n_s$ is the number density of species $s$. The total ion number density $n_i = \sum_s n_s$ equals the electron density only when all ions are singly charged ($Z_s = 1$ for all $s$).

Ion density profiles are commonly inferred from electron density measurements obtained by Thomson scattering or far-infrared interferometry, supplemented by spectroscopic measurements of individual ion species via charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy. Typical core values in tokamak plasmas range from $10^{19}$ to $10^{20}$ m$^{-3}$, decreasing by one to two orders of magnitude toward the scrape-off layer. Combined with [ion_temperature](name:ion_temperature), the ion density determines the ion pressure via $p_i = n_i k_B T_i$, where $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant and $T_i$ is the ion temperature.

candidates.description: Elongation (κ) of each flux surface, defined as the ratio of vertical to horizontal half-axes of the flux surface cross-section.
candidates.reason: base=elongation (shape parameter); locus=of flux_surface (geometry) — shape parameters require explicit surface locus per grammar rules.
candidates.docs_description: Ratio of the vertical half-extent to the horizontal half-extent of a magnetic flux surface cross-section in the poloidal plane, measuring how vertically stretched each nested surface is relative to a circle.
candidates.documentation:
The elongation $\kappa$ is a dimensionless shape parameter that characterizes the vertical-to-horizontal stretching of a nested magnetic flux surface in the poloidal cross-section. It is defined through the semi-axis lengths of the surface contour and equals unity for a perfectly circular cross-section, increasing as the surface becomes progressively more vertically elongated.

For a flux surface approximated by an ellipse, the elongation is

$$
\kappa = \frac{b}{a}
$$

where $b = (Z_{\max} - Z_{\min})/2$ is the vertical semi-axis and $a = (R_{\max} - R_{\min})/2$ is the horizontal semi-axis, with $R$ and $Z$ denoting the major-radius and vertical coordinates in the right-handed cylindrical $(R, \phi, Z)$ frame.

The elongation profile is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction by tracing each flux surface contour in the poloidal plane and extracting its geometric extrema, or directly from MHD equilibrium codes that parameterize the boundary shape.

Near the magnetic axis, $\kappa$ approaches unity for typical up-down symmetric equilibria. At the last closed flux surface, modern shaped tokamaks operate with $\kappa \approx 1.5$–2.0; higher elongation improves energy confinement and MHD stability limits but reduces passive vertical stability. The value at the plasma boundary is captured by [elongation_of_plasma_boundary](name:elongation_of_plasma_boundary), while [triangularity_of_flux_surface](name:triangularity_of_flux_surface) and [squareness_of_flux_surface](name:squareness_of_flux_surface) describe complementary shape features of the same surface.

candidates.description: Electron temperature in the core plasma
candidates.reason: qualifier=electron, base=temperature
candidates.docs_description: Thermal energy of the electron population, expressed in electronvolts, characterizing the width of the electron velocity distribution.
candidates.documentation: The electron temperature $T_e$ quantifies the average kinetic energy per electron in the plasma, defined via $T_e = 2\langle E_e \rangle / (3k_B)$, where $\langle E_e \rangle$ is the mean kinetic energy and $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant. It is a fundamental parameter governing transport, collisionality, and wave–particle interactions. $T_e$ is routinely measured by Thomson scattering, electron cyclotron emission (ECE), and soft X-ray spectroscopy. In the core of H-mode tokamaks, $T_e$ typically ranges from 1–20 keV, dropping to 10–100 eV in the scrape-off layer. The electron temperature is closely related to the [ion temperature](name:ion_temperature) through energy exchange and to the [electron density](name:electron_density) via the [electron pressure](name:electron_pressure) $p_e = n_e k_B T_e$.

candidates.description: Ion temperature averaged over charge states in the core plasma
candidates.reason: qualifier=ion, base=temperature
candidates.docs_description: Mean kinetic energy per degree of freedom of the ion population, expressed as a temperature via the Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution.
candidates.documentation: The ion temperature $T_i$ quantifies the second moment of the ion velocity distribution under the assumption of local thermodynamic equilibrium. It is related to the mean ion kinetic energy by $T_i = \frac{2}{3k_B} \langle E_i \rangle$, where $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant. In fusion plasmas, $T_i$ is routinely measured by charge exchange recombination spectroscopy (CXRS) for main ions and by neutron spectroscopy for fusion-born products. Typical core values range from 1–20 keV in tokamaks, dropping to 10–100 eV in the scrape-off layer. The ion temperature is closely coupled to the [electron temperature](name:electron_temperature) through collisional energy exchange and governs the fusion reaction rate via the [thermal ion pressure](name:thermal_ion_pressure).

candidates.description: Triangularity of the plasma cross-section, measuring the D-shape asymmetry relative to the magnetic axis.
candidates.reason: base=triangularity, covers all triangularity variants (upper, lower, inner) at boundary and separatrix
candidates.docs_description: Dimensionless shape parameter characterizing the D-shaped asymmetry of a poloidal plasma cross-section, defined as the average of the upper and lower triangularities.
candidates.documentation:
Triangularity $\delta$ is a dimensionless shape parameter that quantifies the D-shaped asymmetry of a tokamak plasma cross-section. It is defined as the average of the upper and lower triangularities:

$$
\delta = \frac{\delta_{\rm upper} + \delta_{\rm lower}}{2}
$$

where $\delta_{\rm upper} = (R_0 - R_{\rm upper})/a$ and $\delta_{\rm lower} = (R_0 - R_{\rm lower})/a$, with $R_0$ the geometric center of the plasma boundary, $R_{\rm upper}$ and $R_{\rm lower}$ the major radii of the upper and lower extrema of the boundary, and $a$ the minor radius.

Triangularity is reconstructed from equilibrium codes using magnetic measurements and, when available, internal profile constraints from MSE or polarimetry. In conventional H-mode tokamaks, triangularity values range from 0.3 to 0.5; negative triangularity (reversed D-shape) is also possible and is used in some advanced scenarios. Triangularity is one of the key shape parameters along with [elongation](name:elongation) and [squareness](name:squareness), and it depends on the [major_radius](name:major_radius) and [minor_radius](name:minor_radius) of the plasma boundary.

Sign convention: Positive triangularity corresponds to a D-shaped cross-section with the indentation on the inboard side (i.e., the plasma is more elongated on the outboard side).

candidates.description: Total ion pressure including thermal and fast contributions, summed over charge states
candidates.reason: qualifiers=total,ion, base=pressure
candidates.docs_description: Total pressure from all ion species, including thermal and fast ion populations, summed over charge states.
candidates.documentation:
The total ion pressure is the sum of the partial pressures of all ion species and charge states in the plasma, including both thermal and fast (suprathermal) populations. It represents the kinetic pressure exerted by the ion component and is a key quantity in magnetohydrodynamic equilibrium and transport.

The total ion pressure is defined as:

$$
p_i = \sum_s n_s k_B T_s + p_{\text{fast}}
$$

where $n_s$ and $T_s$ are the density and temperature of each thermal ion species $s$, $k_B$ is the Boltzmann constant, and $p_{\text{fast}}$ is the pressure contribution from fast ions (e.g., neutral-beam-injected or fusion-born alpha particles).

In practice, the thermal ion pressure is obtained from measured ion temperature and density profiles (e.g., charge exchange recombination spectroscopy, Thomson scattering), while the fast ion pressure is inferred from neutron emission, neutral particle analysis, or equilibrium reconstruction. Typical core values range from $10^4$ to $10^6$ Pa in high-performance tokamak plasmas, depending on heating power and confinement regime.

See also [thermal_ion_pressure](name:thermal_ion_pressure), [fast_ion_pressure](name:fast_ion_pressure), [electron_pressure](name:electron_pressure), and [total_pressure](name:total_pressure).

candidates.description: Ratio of plasma pressure to magnetic pressure, a key MHD equilibrium parameter
candidates.reason: base=beta, qualifier=plasma, covers poloidal, toroidal, and normalized beta variants
candidates.docs_description: Dimensionless ratio of the plasma pressure to the magnetic pressure, quantifying the efficiency of magnetic confinement in fusion plasmas.
candidates.documentation:
Plasma beta is the fundamental dimensionless parameter that measures the ratio of the kinetic pressure of the plasma to the magnetic pressure exerted by the confining field. It indicates how efficiently the magnetic field confines the plasma: higher beta means more plasma pressure is sustained for a given magnetic field strength.

The defining relation is

$$
\beta = \frac{p}{B^2 / (2\mu_0)}
$$

where $p$ is the total plasma pressure (sum of thermal and fast-particle contributions), $B$ is the magnetic field magnitude, and $\mu_0$ is the vacuum permeability.

In conventional tokamaks, the volume-averaged toroidal beta typically ranges from 1% to 10%, while spherical tokamaks can achieve values exceeding 40%. The local beta can vary across the plasma cross-section. Plasma beta is routinely inferred from equilibrium reconstruction codes that combine magnetic measurements with kinetic profiles of density and temperature. It is closely related to the normalized beta $\beta_N = \beta / (I_p / a B_t)$ and to the poloidal beta $\beta_p = 8\pi \langle p \rangle / B_p^2$, where $B_p$ is the poloidal magnetic field. See also [plasma_pressure](name:plasma_pressure), [magnetic_pressure](name:magnetic_pressure), [toroidal_beta](name:toroidal_beta), [poloidal_beta](name:poloidal_beta), and [beta_normalized](name:beta_normalized).

candidates.description: Magnetic shear (s) defined as (ρ_tor/q)·(dq/dρ_tor), quantifying the local radial variation of the safety factor
candidates.reason: base=magnetic_shear, dimensionless radial profile of safety factor gradient
candidates.docs_description: Local radial variation of the safety factor, defined as the normalized gradient of the safety factor with respect to the normalized toroidal flux radius.
candidates.documentation:
Magnetic shear quantifies the rate of change of the safety factor across the plasma radius, characterizing the twist of magnetic field lines. It is a key parameter for MHD stability, particularly for tearing modes and sawteeth.

The magnetic shear is defined as:

$$
s = \frac{\rho_{\mathrm{tor}}}{q} \frac{dq}{d\rho_{\mathrm{tor}}}
$$

where $\rho_{\mathrm{tor}}$ is the normalized toroidal flux radius (0 at magnetic axis, 1 at plasma boundary) and $q$ is the [safety factor](name:safety_factor).

Magnetic shear is derived from the safety factor profile, which is obtained from equilibrium reconstruction using magnetic measurements and constraints from polarimetry or motional Stark effect. It can also be inferred from the pitch of magnetic field lines.

In standard H-mode tokamak plasmas, magnetic shear is positive and ranges from 0.5 to 2 in the core, increasing to 3–5 near the edge. Negative shear (reversed shear) occurs in advanced scenarios with a non-monotonic $q$ profile, often used for improved confinement.

Sign convention: Positive when the safety factor increases with increasing minor radius (normal positive shear). Negative when the safety factor decreases radially (reversed shear).

candidates.description: Safety factor (q) radial profile, ratio of toroidal to poloidal magnetic field line turns per flux surface.
candidates.reason: base=safety_factor, no qualifiers, no locus.
candidates.docs_description: Number of toroidal transits a magnetic field line makes per single poloidal circuit of a given flux surface.
candidates.documentation:
The safety factor $q$ is the number of toroidal transits a magnetic field line makes per single poloidal circuit of a given flux surface. It quantifies the helicity of the magnetic field and is a key parameter for MHD stability.

The safety factor is defined as the derivative of the toroidal magnetic flux with respect to the poloidal magnetic flux:

$$
q(\psi) = \frac{d\Phi_{\rm tor}}{d\Phi_{\rm pol}}
$$

where $\Phi_{\rm tor}$ is the toroidal magnetic flux and $\Phi_{\rm pol}$ is the poloidal magnetic flux enclosed by the flux surface. In a large-aspect-ratio circular tokamak, $q \approx (r B_\phi)/(R B_\theta)$ with $r$ the minor radius, $R$ the major radius, $B_\phi$ the toroidal field, and $B_\theta$ the poloidal field.

The safety factor profile is reconstructed from equilibrium codes using external magnetic measurements combined with internal constraints from motional Stark effect (MSE) or polarimetry. The on-axis value $q_0$ and the edge value $q_{95}$ are routinely reported.

In conventional H-mode tokamaks, $q_0 \approx 1$ and $q_{95} \approx 3$–$5$. Regions with $q < 1$ are susceptible to sawtooth instabilities. The safety factor is derived from the [poloidal_magnetic_flux](name:poloidal_magnetic_flux) and [toroidal_magnetic_flux](name:toroidal_magnetic_flux) profiles. Its radial derivative, [magnetic_shear](name:magnetic_shear), influences stability.

candidates.description: Total electron number density including thermal and non-thermal contributions
candidates.reason: qualifier=electron, base=density
candidates.docs_description: Number of free electrons per unit volume in the plasma, including both thermal and non-thermal populations.
candidates.documentation:
The electron density $n_e$ is the zeroth velocity moment of the electron distribution function $f_e(\mathbf{v})$, giving the total number of free electrons per unit volume:

$$
n_e = \int f_e(\mathbf{v}) \, d^3v
$$

It is routinely measured by Thomson scattering, interferometry, and reflectometry. In the core of magnetically confined plasmas, $n_e$ ranges from $10^{19}$ to $10^{20}$ m$^{-3}$, dropping by one to two orders of magnitude in the scrape-off layer. The electron density is closely related to the [electron temperature](name:electron_temperature) via the plasma pressure $p_e = n_e k_B T_e$, and to the [plasma current](name:plasma_current) through the Spitzer conductivity. Quasi-neutrality links it to the [ion density](name:ion_density) in the bulk plasma.

candidates.description: Total electron pressure including thermal and non-thermal contributions
candidates.reason: qualifiers=total,electron, base=pressure
candidates.docs_description: Total pressure contributed by the electron population, including both thermal (Maxwellian) and non-thermal (e.g., runaway, fast) components.
candidates.documentation:
The total electron pressure $p_{e,\mathrm{total}}$ is the sum of the thermal electron pressure $p_{e,\mathrm{th}} = n_e k_B T_e$ and the non-thermal electron pressure $p_{e,\mathrm{non\text{-}th}}$ arising from suprathermal or runaway populations:

$$
p_{e,\mathrm{total}} = p_{e,\mathrm{th}} + p_{e,\mathrm{non\text{-}th}}
$$

where $n_e$ is the electron density, $T_e$ the electron temperature, and $k_B$ the Boltzmann constant. The thermal component is routinely measured by Thomson scattering or electron cyclotron emission, while the non-thermal component is inferred from hard X-ray spectra, synchrotron radiation, or equilibrium reconstruction. In H-mode tokamak plasmas, the core total electron pressure typically ranges from 0.1 to 1 MPa, with the non-thermal fraction becoming significant during runaway-electron plateaus or strong auxiliary heating. See also [thermal_electron_pressure](name:thermal_electron_pressure), [fast_electron_pressure](name:fast_electron_pressure), and [total_pressure](name:total_pressure).

candidates.description: Toroidal component of the magnetic field evaluated at the magnetic axis
candidates.reason: projection=toroidal component, base=magnetic_field, locus=at magnetic_axis
candidates.docs_description: Toroidal component of the magnetic field at the magnetic axis, the innermost point of the nested flux surfaces. It is a fundamental parameter determining the magnetic confinement and MHD stability of the plasma.
candidates.documentation:
The toroidal magnetic field at the magnetic axis, often denoted $B_0$, is the value of the toroidal component $B_\phi$ at the point where the poloidal magnetic field vanishes and the flux surfaces are concentric. It sets the scale for the magnetic confinement and the gyrofrequency of charged particles.

The on-axis toroidal field is related to the toroidal field coil current $I_\text{TF}$ and the major radius $R_0$ of the magnetic axis by the vacuum approximation:

$$
B_0 = \frac{\mu_0 I_\text{TF}}{2\pi R_0}
$$

where $\mu_0$ is the vacuum permeability. In a plasma, diamagnetic and paramagnetic effects modify the field slightly, but the vacuum value remains a good approximation at the axis.

The on-axis toroidal field is routinely inferred from equilibrium reconstruction codes that fit magnetic measurements (flux loops, pickup coils) and kinetic profiles. It can also be directly computed from the toroidal field coil current and the geometry of the [major_radius_of_magnetic_axis](name:major_radius_of_magnetic_axis).

Typical values range from 1–6 T in present-day tokamaks (e.g., 2.25 T in DIII-D, 3.5 T in JET, 5.2 T in ASDEX Upgrade) and up to 12 T in ITER. In stellarators, the on-axis field is similarly determined by the coil set.

Sign convention: Positive when $B_\phi$ points in the direction of increasing toroidal angle $\phi$ (counter-clockwise when viewed from above).

candidates.description: Total number density of a given ion species, including thermal and fast contributions.
candidates.reason: subject=ion, base=density
candidates.docs_description: Total number density of a specific ion species, including all energy populations (thermal and fast).
candidates.documentation:
The ion density $n_i$ is the number of ions of a given species per unit volume. It is a fundamental quantity in plasma physics, determining fusion reaction rates, particle transport, and plasma pressure.

The ion density is the zeroth moment of the ion velocity distribution function $f_i(\mathbf{v})$:

$$
n_i = \int f_i(\mathbf{v}) \, d^3v
$$

where the integral is over all velocity space. For a Maxwellian distribution, $n_i$ is related to the ion pressure $p_i$ and temperature $T_i$ by $p_i = n_i k_B T_i$.

Ion densities are routinely measured using charge-exchange recombination spectroscopy (CXRS) for main ions and impurities, or inferred from electron density measurements combined with effective charge $Z_{\text{eff}}$ from bremsstrahlung. In the plasma core, hydrogenic ion densities typically range from $10^{19}$ to $10^{20}$ m$^{-3}$, while impurity densities are one to three orders of magnitude lower. In the scrape-off layer, densities drop to $10^{18}$–$10^{19}$ m$^{-3}$.

See also [electron_density](name:electron_density) for the electron counterpart and [ion_temperature](name:ion_temperature) for the thermal energy of the same species.

candidates.description: Elongation (κ) of flux surfaces, the ratio of vertical to horizontal half-axis
candidates.reason: base=elongation, dimensionless shape parameter
candidates.docs_description: Elongation (κ) of a flux surface, defined as the ratio of its vertical half-axis to its horizontal half-axis.
candidates.documentation: Elongation quantifies the vertical stretching of a flux surface relative to its width. For a flux surface with vertical half-axis $b$ and horizontal half-axis $a$, the elongation is $\kappa = b/a$. In tokamak plasmas, elongation typically ranges from 1 (circular cross-section) to about 2 in highly shaped configurations, with values around 1.6–1.8 common in H-mode. Elongation is inferred from equilibrium reconstruction using magnetic measurements and constraints from diagnostics such as motional Stark effect or polarimetry. Higher elongation improves confinement but can reduce vertical stability, requiring feedback control. See also [triangularity](name:triangularity), [safety_factor](name:safety_factor), and [plasma_shape](name:plasma_shape).
